coredata.dc:title: One step electro-oxidative preparation of graphene quantum dots from wood charcoal as a peroxidase mimetic
coredata.dc:creator: Nirala, Narsingh R.
Khandelwal, Gaurav
Kumar, Brijesh
Vinita,
Prakash, Rajiv
Kumar, Vinod
coredata.dc:description:
In present study, we highlight one-step electrochemical synthesis of nearly uniform size (~ 5nm) of graphene quantum dots (E-GQDs) from wood charcoal and their further application as a peroxidase enzyme mimetic. The structural and optical properties of as-synthesized E-GQDs were probed by TEM, AFM, and spectroscopic techniques. Peroxidase enzyme mimetic potential of E-GQDs were examined for colorimetric detection of H2O2 and glucose. E-GQDs allowed a rapid and sensitive detection of glucose with a detection limit of 0.006mM for dynamic response range of 0.01–0.6mM. The calculated higher value of V
max (7.2 × 10−7 Ms−1) along with lower K
m (0.012mM) corroborate enhanced the peroxidase-like activity of E-GQDs. Study introduces a cheap and widely available raw material for the electrochemical synthesis of graphene quantum dots with commendable enzyme mimetic activity which may have a huge impact in developing calorimetric bioanalysis systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: E-GQDs
Horse radish peroxidase (HRP)
Glucose oxidase (GOx)
Colorimetric
Peroxidase mimetic

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical synthesis of graphene quantum dots (E-GQDs)
Evaluation of peroxidase mimetic catalytic activity of E-GQDs
Kinetic study of the detection of Glucose and H2O2
Selectivity of the proposed colorimetric system
Reproducibility and repeatability of the proposed colorimetric system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic showing synthesis procedure of E-GQDs.
Characterization of E-GQDs, (a) UV–Vis spectra, (b) FTIR spectra, (c) TEM micrograph, (d) AFM.

Time-dependent absorbance changes at 652nm of TMB solution in different reaction systems. (a) TMB + H2O2, (b) E-GQDs + TMB, and (c) E-GQDs + TMB + H2O2. The inset shows the photos of the reaction system (a), (b), and (c), respectively. The concentration of E-GQDs, TMB, and H2O2 are 0.1µgmL−1, 0.5mM, and 0.02mM, respectively.

Peroxidase like activity of E-GQDs dependency on (a) H2O2 concentration, (b) pH, and (c) temperature. Experiments were carried out using 0.1µgmL−1 E-GQDs in 5mL of 0.2molL−1 PBS with TMB (5mM) as substrate. 0.5mM of H2O2 at pH 3.5 was used at 35°C.

Steady state kinetic assay and catalytic mechanism of E-GQDs. (a) The concentration of H2O2 was 50mM for E-GQDs and the TMB concentration was varied, (b) the concentration of TMB was 0.8mM form E-GQDs and the H2O2 concentration was varied. The velocity (v) of the reaction was measured using 0.1μgmL−1 E-GQDs in 10mL of 0.2molL−1 NaAc buffer at pH 3.5 at 35°C. The error bars represent the standard error derived from three repeated measurements.
Schematic illustration of oxidation reaction of TMB catalyzed by E-GQDs.

(a) UV–Visible abruption spectra showing interaction of GQDs + TMB + GOx with different concentration of glucose (0–0.6mM), (b) Calibration curve showing detection of glucose. Error bars represent the standard deviation for three measurements.

(a) A dose–response curve for H2O2 detection and the inset of (a) are the images of colored products for different concentrations of H2O2 (0–250µM), (b) Calibration curve for H2O2 detection. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements.

Selectivity of glucose detection. All measurements were performed in 0.5mL of 0.15molL−1 NaH2PO4 buffer solutions (pH 3.5) containing 1mM TMB, 2µgmL−1 E-GQDs and 1 mgmL−1 glucose oxidase. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three-time measurements.
Results of glucose determination in serum sample.

(a) Intra-day and (b) inter-day repeatability test results of EGQDs (2 µgmL−1) mediated detection of glucose (with concentration ranges between 0.01mM to 0.6mM). The error bars represent the standard deviation of triplicate measurements.
Comparison of various nanoparticles- based methods for detection of glucose.

head.ce:title: One step electro-oxidative preparation of graphene quantum dots from wood charcoal as a peroxidase mimetic

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Zoology, Banaras Hindu University, Varanasi 221005, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Zoology, Banaras Hindu University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Indian Institute of Technology Delhi, Hauzkhas, New Delhi 110 016, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Dr. A.P.J. Abdul Kalam Technical University, Lucknow 226021, UP, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dr. A.P.J. Abdul Kalam Technical University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Technology, IIT (BHU), Varanasi 221005, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Technology, IIT (BHU)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Engineering, Ben Gurion University of the Negev, Beer-Sheva 84105, Israel

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Engineering, Ben Gurion University of the Negev

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
In present study, we highlight one-step electrochemical synthesis of nearly uniform size (~ 5nm) of graphene quantum dots (E-GQDs) from wood charcoal and their further application as a peroxidase enzyme mimetic. The structural and optical properties of as-synthesized E-GQDs were probed by TEM, AFM, and spectroscopic techniques. Peroxidase enzyme mimetic potential of E-GQDs were examined for colorimetric detection of H2O2 and glucose. E-GQDs allowed a rapid and sensitive detection of glucose with a detection limit of 0.006mM for dynamic response range of 0.01–0.6mM. The calculated higher value of V
max (7.2 × 10−7 Ms−1) along with lower K
m (0.012mM) corroborate enhanced the peroxidase-like activity of E-GQDs. Study introduces a cheap and widely available raw material for the electrochemical synthesis of graphene quantum dots with commendable enzyme mimetic activity which may have a huge impact in developing calorimetric bioanalysis systems.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past few decades considerable efforts have been made to develop artificial enzyme mimetics with structural and functional similarities to those natural enzymes [1]. Artificial enzymes are very stable in extreme conditions and their cost of production is low and not complicated [1]. So far wide range of artificial enzyme mimetic have been explored, Hemin, [2] porphyrin [3] and DNAzymes [4] are some of the examples. Horseradish peroxidase (HRP), is one the best-studied example of peroxidase mimetic enzyme [5]. HRP catalyzes and converts its substrate into colorimetric end product, due to this peculiar property it has been extensively used in enzyme linked sorbent assay (ELISA) based disease diagnosis [6]. Like other natural enzymes, the expensive preparation/purification steps involved in the production of HRP along with its extreme environmental sensitivity limits its potential applications [7–9]. To overcome these challenges considerable efforts have been made to explore the HRP activity of nanomaterials. So far a variety of nanomaterials have been tested for their peroxidase like activity. For examples, nanostructures of Fe3O4 [10] , Co3O4 [11] etc. More recently the peroxidase activity of carbon nanomaterials have been studied [5,12,13]. Among carbon nanomaterials the peroxidase activity of single walled carbon nanotubes, [14,15] graphene oxide [15] carbon dots [16] and their composite [17–20] have been studied. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) a new kind of carbon nanomaterials, have received increasing attention due to their extraordinary physico-chemical properties such as high surface area, improved solubility, biocompatibility and stable fluorescence with adjustable bandgap [21]. Due to these unique properties GQDs have been thoroughly investigated in biomedical applications [22]. However, the enzymatic properties of GQDs are not well explored, only a few published reports are there which describe their enzymatic activity [23–25]. In most of the earlier reported work, adopted synthesis methodologies were complex and time consuming and the used source material was itself expensive. In addition, these methods were not environmentally friendly due to the use of strong acids in many cases [26]. On the other hand the electrochemical methods for nanomaterials synthesis have emerged as a promising alternative because it is quick and does not need any harsh chemicals [19,20]. In current study, we are reporting single step facile electrochemical synthesis of graphene quantum dots (E-GQDs) using a cheap and widely available carbon source i.e. wood charcoal. The peroxidase like activity of electrochemically synthesized graphene quantum dots (E-GQDs) have been thoroughly investigated and further exploited for developing a colorimetric biosensor for the detection of H2O2 and glucose simultaneously.

Wood charcoal, glucose oxidase, and glucose were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, India. H2SO4 (98%) and HNO3 (69–72%) were obtained from SD fine Chem. Ltd. Mumbai, India. 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), Sodium carbonate (Na2CO3), disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4) and monobasic sodium phosphate (NaH2PO4) were purchased from Fischer Scientific India Pvt. Ltd. All chemical reagents used were of analytical grade. Dialysis bag was procured from Millipore Merck Limited, Mumbai, India. Double distilled water was used throughout the experiment. All reagents were used as received.

The structural and morphological analysis of synthesized E-GQDs were done by suing FEI Tecnai-G2 transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Spectroscopic analysis was carried out by a Perkin Elmer UV–Visible-Lambda 25 spectrophotometer and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy (Perkin Elmer Spectrum 65, FT-IR spectrometer). Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) measurements were done by using a Multimode Nanoscope V scanning probe microscopy system (Bruker, USA). The specimen was prepared by drop-casting the aqueous suspensions of E-GQDs on a freshly cleaned silicon wafer and dried in air.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical synthesis of graphene quantum dots (E-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical oxidation of wood charcoal was carried out using a conventional two-electrode system (Pt as a counter electrode) with a CH Instrument (Model-705). 5mL aqueous solution of 0.01molL−1 of [(NH4)2S2O8] at pH 7.0 was used as an electrolyte and the oxidation was carried out with the current intensity in the range of 100–200mAcm−2 at 5V of potential. The free radicals such as ̊O2 and ̊OH produced by anodic oxidation of water, worked as electrochemical “scissors” to oxidize large graphene sheets of charcoal into very small (nano-dimensions) oxidized sheets of multi layered graphene (graphene quantum dots). Top solution containing synthesized E-GQDs were decanted carefully and centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 20min to remove the insoluble residues. The solution was further dialyzed using a dialysis bag with retained molecular weight: 2000Da for 3 days to finally get E-GQDs.

5mM stock solution of 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) was prepared in ethanol (anhydrous). 0.2molL−1 of Phosphate buffered saline (PBS) was prepared and its pH was adjusted to 3.7. For detection of H2O2 by E-GQDs, 10μL of E-GQDs (0.2µgmL−1) were added to 150μL of PBS having 20μL of different concentrations of H2O2 (ranging from 0µM to 250µM). 20μL of TMB (mM) was added to the above reaction mixture and incubated for 5min. The absorbance of the oxidized TMB was recorded at 652nm. For glucose detection, 5μL of glucose oxidase (GOx) with 50μL of different concentrations of glucose (0.02mM to 0.6mM.) in PBS (0.5mM, pH 7.0) were incubated for 30min at 37°C. To this solution, 10μL of E-GQDs (0.2µgmL−1) and 150μL of PBS (0.2molL−1, pH 3.7) were added. The measurement was started by addition of 20μL of TMB (5mM). The change in the color of the product (each for 5min) was measured at 652nm.

E-GQDs were prepared by electro-oxidation of wood charcoal. The synthesis procedure has been shown with help of a schematic in Fig. 1. Basically two facts are responsible for the efficient direct electrochemical exfoliation of wood charcoal into small sized GQDs without hampering sp2 bonded carbon network. First is the efficient electrochemical oxidative cleavage of wood charcoal into multilayered graphene sheets [26,27]. Secondly the used electrolyte (NH4)2S2O8, which is very important for generating the specific GQDs. It is reported that produced S2O8 2− ions produced at a large anodic voltage discharges SO4 2− which work as strong oxidizing radicals [28]. Thus, in our case, SO4• − radicals are also produced by the electrochemical reaction of S2O8 2− [29]. And it is believed that SO4•− radicals may function as sharp electrochemical scissors to cut down graphene sheets into very small intact sp2 structures namely quantum dots by oxidizing the C–C bonds. Thus, the role of (NH4)2S2O8, is very clear that it produces strong free radicals which are key towards the formation of E-GQDs.

The optical properties of synthesized E-GQDs were initially studied by using UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy ( Fig. 2a). The absorption peak in case of E-GQDs as seen at λ
max 225nm is due to the π-π* transition of C˭C bonds of E-GQDs. Apart from absorption peak at λ
max 225nm, one more shoulder peak at λ
max 290nm is due to the n-π* transition of C˭O bonds of E-GQDs. Excitation dependent PL behaviors of E-GQDs were recorded (Fig. S1). A sharp emission at λ
max em. 450nm corresponding to λ
max ex. 325nm is noticed in PL spectra. However, λ
max em. below and above to λ
max ex. 325nm were reduced in PL intensity and appearance of another broad peak at λ
max em. 525nm is noticed. The Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra (Fig. 2b) were used to identify the surface functional groups of E-GQDs. The peak at 1660cm−1 was attributed to C˭O stretching vibrations of carboxylic groups and 1390cm−1 was assigned to (C-O) present in the carboxylic group. The peak found at 1112cm−1 was due to epoxide (C-O-C), peaks at 3436cm−1 was assigned to -OH functional groups. These oxygen-containing functional groups (shown in FTIR spectra of E-GQDs) were introduced to the edges and onto the basal plane were aroused during electrochemical oxidation of charcoal into E-GQDs.

Raman spectroscopic characterization of the synthesized E-GQDs was also carried out (Fig. S2). The Raman spectrum of E-GQDs showed a D-band at 1363cm−1 and a G band at 1582cm−1. The calculated value of ID/IG was found to be 0.85, which is good agreement with the earlier reports [30]. The high ID/IG designates the increased number of the sp2 cluster to arise due to a different degree of oxidation. Fig. 2c showed the TEM micrograph of the synthesized E-GQDs. The size of the E-GQDs ranged between 3 and 9nm with an average size of 6nm. HRTEM (Inset of Fig. 2a) shows parallel lines of graphite with a spacing of 3.4Å an indicative of crystallinity of E-GQDs.

Further, AFM study was also carried out to probe the size and height profile of the E-GQDs (Fig. 2d). In height profile graph, the average height of E-GQDs were measured to be 6nm, which was accordance to the observation made in TEM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of peroxidase mimetic catalytic activity of E-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The peroxidase-like activity of E-GQDs has firstly evaluated by the catalytic oxidation of peroxidase substrate (TMB) in the presence of H2O2. Upon addition of E-GQDs and H2O2 to TMB solution, a typical blue color reaction was observed and the characteristic absorbance of reaction solution at 652nm ( Fig. 3), which were attributed to the charge-transfer complexes derived from the one-electron oxidation of TMB [33,34], increased with the increasing of reaction time (Fig. 3). However, only addition of H2O2 or E-GQDs to TMB solution the absorbance at 652nm displayed negligible change (Fig. 3a), indicating no oxidation reaction occurred. These results indicated that E-GQDs exhibited intrinsic peroxidase-like activity and both E-GQDs and H2O2 were required for the reaction.

The peroxidase-like activity of E-GQDs were optimized by selecting the substrates TMB and H2O2 as a model reaction system. pH, temperature and H2O2 concentration dependent catalytic activity of E-GQDs have been measured ( Fig. 4). The catalytic activity was increased with the reaction time and became stable after 18min (Fig. 3). In earlier reports, it was noticed that catalytic activity of HRP was largely inhibited after incubation at pH lower than 4 or temperature higher than 50°C [11]. In contrast, E-GQDs exhibited high catalytic activity over a wide range of pH (1−10) (Fig. 4b) and temperature (25–75°C) (Fig. 4c), the robustness of E-GQDs make them potentially applicable under harsh conditions. Like HRP and other nanomaterials-based peroxidase mimics [15,31–34] the catalytic activity of E-GQDs was inhibited at a high H2O2 concentration (Fig. 4a). Under our experimental conditions, the optimal temperature, reaction time were measured at 55°C, 18min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetic study of the detection of Glucose and H2O2
ce:section.ce:para:
To explore the catalytic mechanism of E-GQDs, kinetic studies were performed. The steady-state kinetics parameters were chosen for the study of its catalytic activity by varying the concentration of TMB or H2O2 while keeping the others constant. The peroxidase-like activity of the E-GQDs was determined by the change in color of the reaction with changing the concentrations of TMB and H2O2 in this system respectively. The initial reaction rate was determined by using the Beer–Lambert Law (Eq. (1)):
(1)
C
=
A
Ɛ b
where c is the substrate concentration, A is the absorbance, b is the thickness of the solution. The absorbance data were back-calculated to give concentrations using a molar absorption coefficient, Ɛ, of 39,000M−1cm−1 for the TMB, derived oxidation products which are high concentration to other any nanomaterials [18]. Reaction rates at different concentrations of the substrate were determined by calculating the slopes of absorbance with time. For our study, a typical Michaelis–Menten curves were seen for both TMB and H2O2. The Michaelis–Menten constant (K m) and maximum initial velocity (V max) were obtained from Michaelis–Menten equation ( Fig. 5, Eq. (2)):
(2)
ν
o
=
V
max
[ S ]
K
m
+ [ S ]
Where νo is initial velocity, Vmax is maximum initial velocity, Km = Vmax /2, [S]=substrate concentration. Smaller the value of Km, stronger is the affinity between enzyme and substrate, resulting into the more efficient catalyst. Higher Vmax, suggested that higher catalytic towards TMB in presence of H2O2. The Km value for E-GQDs were found to be higher in the case of H2O2 (7mM) than HRP as shown in Fig. 5b, whereas in the case of TMBs, it was found to be smaller than HRP (0.009mM) as shown in Fig. 5a. These results were credited to the fact that initially when the concentration of TMB was fixed, thus the number of active sites that remains fixed, in turn limiting the catalytic activity hence higher the obtained Km value. In second step the increased concentration of TMB maximized the number active sites resulting in the enhanced catalytic activity and lowering Km value. The observed high catalytic activity is attributed to the small size of E-GQDs having a higher density of functional groups and unpaired electrons on its edges. Density functional theory describes that the functional groups at of graphene surfaces play a significant role in the reduction reaction of H2O2 [16]. In our case, we assumed the existence of similar effect at the edges of heavily functionalized E-GQDs. In addition the catalytic activity in E-GQDs were most probably originated from an increase in the electron density and mobility on the surface. Due to electron transfer from lone-pair of electrons in TMB amino groups to the E-GQDs. This would result in the acceleration of electron transfer from the E-GQDs to H2O2, and the reaction rate of TMB oxidation by H2O2 would increase. Fig. 6, is a schematic illustration of the catalytic activity of E-GQDs.

H2O2 is the main product of glucose oxidase (GOx) catalyzed reaction. Glucose detection could be realized when combined with GOx ( Fig. 7). GOx catalyzed the oxidation of glucose to gluconic acid (in PBS) and oxygen in solution was converted to H2O2 which in turn oxidized TMB to produce a blue-color product via the catalysis of E-GQDs. Fig. 7a showed the dependence of the absorbance at 652nm on the concentration of glucose. The linear range for glucose was obtained from 0.01 to 0.1mM (R2~0.97) with the detection limit of 0.006mM (Fig. 7b).

Further, the color variation from pale yellow to blue was obvious on visual observation (inset of Fig. 7a), offering a convenient approach to detect glucose by the naked eyes even at low concentrations. The obtained results of our study are well comparable to with earlier reports ( Table 1).

The catalytic activity of E-GQDs were further used for developing a colorimetric method for the detection of H2O2. Fig. 8 shows the catalytic activity of E-GQDs with increased concentration of H2O2. Fig. 8a indicates the dependence of the absorbance at 652nm on the concentration of H2O2 under optimal conditions. The absorbance increased with the increasing of H2O2 concentration from 0 to 250µM. A linear relationship between the absorbance and H2O2 concentration from 0 to 0 250µM (R2 ~ 0.99) was obtained with a detection limit of 0.9µM (Fig. 8b).

Recent progress in nanomaterials based enzyme mimetic have created huge interest in the scientific community to further explore more efficient enzyme mimetic. Owing to their excellent catalytic properties, carbon nanomaterials specially carbon nanotubes, graphene have been extensively studied to mimic various natural enzymes [35]. The emergence of GQDs, a new kind of carbon nanomaterials with the extraordinary properties have provided new opportunities to look more into its catalytic activities [22]. However, enzyme mimetic studies on GQDs have been less explored, particularly in terms of novel synthetic strategies. Earlier reported methods are quite challenging particularly in terms of synthesis. For example, Pan et al. synthesized GQDs with blue luminescence by a hydrothermal from pre-oxidized graphene sheets [36]. Qu et al., used graphene oxide for the synthesis of GQDs by cyclic voltammetry (CV) within the potential window ~ 3V in PBS solution [37]. Mullen et al., described a bottom up approach for the synthesis of GQDs of larger size using polycyclic aromatic hydro-carbons [38], however, no control on edge states were reported. E- beam based synthesis of GQDs are also reported [39]. Further, synthesis of GQDs by transformation of C60 catalyzed by ruthenium is reported [40]. Most of the above reported methods are limited by the need of special equipment using expensive source material and the yield is very low. Therefore, electrochemical approach [41] with a potentiostat (with specific oxidation/reduction) would be more advantageous to obtain GQDs with more precise control over shape and size. Use of an inexpensive and easily available carbon source i.e. wood charcoal, and a facile electrochemical method (without using any harsh chemical) for synthesizing graphene quantum dots make this study attractive for thorough investigation of other properties of GQDs. In addition, the efficient peroxidase mimetic application of synthesized E-GQDs extends their role in bio- analytical purposes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the proposed colorimetric system
ce:section.ce:para: Fructose, cholesterol, ascorbic acids, urea, and cysteine were chosen to investigate the selectivity of the develop colorimetric method, the result ( Fig. 9) demonstrated specificity for glucose detection. The colorimetric method was also applied to detect glucose in serum samples ( Fig. 10), obtained results were in good agreement with those obtained by using the conventional enzymatic method (Table S1).

The selectivity described here was tested with glucose and other interference like uric acid, urea, ascorbic acid, cysteine and glucose which is generally present in blood, each at 1mM. The concentration was 0.6mM for glucose, 2mM for cholesterol, urea, ascorbic acid, cysteine, and glucose. This result clearly demonstrated that this E-GQDs based method showcased high selectivity towards glucose detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility and repeatability of the proposed colorimetric system
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S3a demonstrate reproducible results of the proposed system when the experiments were repeated for five times under same set of conditions. Fig. S3a showed consistent repeatability of the results for five samples in similar experimental conditions. Enzyme mimetic potential of EGQDs were further verified by performing both intra (after 0, 1, 3, 5, 7 and 12h) and inter-day (after 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 days) repeatability test of glucose detection (with a ranges of concentrations i.e. 0.01mM to 0.6mM of glucose). Fig. 11a and b are showing the results of intra and inter-day repeatability test of glucose detection respectively. The reliable repeatability results further validate the efficient HRP potential of EGQDs.

In conclusion, we have reported the synthesis of graphene quantum dots by electro-oxidation of wood charcoal, a cheap and widely available source materials. Further, the peroxidase like catalytic activity of synthesized graphene quantum dots (E-GQDs) was studied. E-GQDs catalyzed the oxidation of TMB by H2O2 to produce the typical blue color reaction and the catalytic activity was strongly dependent on catalyst concentration, H2O2 concentration, pH, and temperature. Catalytic properties of E-GQDs followed the typical Michaelis–Menten kinetics. Based on these finding, colorimetric methods for the detection of H2O2 and glucose were developed. It is envisioned that our work would open up the possibility of utilizing E-GQDs as enzymatic mimics in biotechnology.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: NRN acknowledges the financial assistance from UGC (RJNF-JRF-SC-252), SERB-NPDF (PDF/2016/000243). Central instrumentation facilities of Biophysics, Department of Physics of Banaras Hindu University, Varanasi, India is highly acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2017.05.061.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanomaterials with enzyme-like characteristics (nanozymes): next-generation artificial enzymes

Carbon nanodots as peroxidase mimetics and their applications to glucose detection

SDS–MoS2 nanoparticles as highly-efficient peroxidase mimetics for colorimetric detection of H2O2 and glucose
High peroxidase-like activity of iron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots and its application in immunosorbent assay

Focusing on luminescent graphene quantum dots: current status and future perspectives
Functional graphene nanomaterials based architectures: bio interactions, fabrications, and emerging biological applications
Graphene oxide derived graphene quantum dots with different photoluminescence properties and peroxidase-like catalytic activity
Intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic activity of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and their application in the colorimetric detection of H2O2 and glucose
Colorimetric detection of cholesterol based on highly efficient peroxidase mimetic activity of graphene quantum dots
Facile synthesis of water-soluble, highly fluorescent graphene quantum dots as a robust biological label for stem cells

Facile, rapid and upscaled synthesis of green luminescent functional graphene quantum dots for bioimaging
Highly-efficient peroxidase-like catalytic activity of graphene dots for biosensing
A sensitive H2O2 assay based on dumbbell‐like PtPd‐Fe3O4 nanoparticles

Carbon-based nanomaterials for nanozymes, nanozymes: next wave of artificial enzymes

Transforming C60 molecules into graphene quantum dots

Glucose oxidase–graphene–chitosan modified electrode for direct electrochemistry and glucose sensing
Highly specific-glucose fluorescence sensing based on boronic anthraquinone derivatives via the GOx enzymatic reaction
Luminescent CePO4:Tb colloids for H2O2 and glucose sensing
Visual detection of blood glucose based on peroxidase-like activity of WS2 nanosheets
